statement.focus_question: Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to $k\sigma(k)=n$, where $\sigma(k)$ is the sum of divisors of $k$. Is it true that $f(n)\leq n^{o(\frac{1}{\log\log n})}$?
statement.normalized: Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to $k\sigma(k)=n$, where $\sigma(k)$ is the sum of divisors of $k$. Is it true that $f(n)\leq n^{o(\frac{1}{\log\log n})}$? Perhaps even $\leq (\log n)^{O(1)}$?
statement.original: Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to $k\sigma(k)=n$, where $\sigma(k)$ is the sum of divisors of $k$. Is it true that $f(n)\leq n^{o(\frac{1}{\log\log n})}$? Perhaps even $\leq (\log n)^{O(1)}$?
